Feelings Aren't Facts

At this point you may be asking yourself, ‘‘

you may be asking yourself, ‘‘Okay. I un-

48 David D. Burns, M.D.

as clearly and realistically as th

learly and realistically as the next person, so if what I
am telling myself is irrational, why does it seem so right?’’

Even though your depressing thoughts may be distorted,
they

houghts may be distorted,
they nevertheless create a powerful illusion of truth. Let me
expose the basis for the deception in blunt terms—your
feelings are not facts! In fact, your

s are not facts! In fact, your feelings, per se, don’t
even count—except as a mirror of the way you are thinking.
If your perceptions make no sense, the feelings they create
will b

he feelings they create
will be as absurd as the images reflected in the trick mirrors
at an amusement park. But these abnormal emotions feel
just as valid and realistic as the gen

valid and realistic as the genuine feelings created by
undistorted thoughts, so you automatically attribute truth to
them. This is why depression is such a powerful form of
mental

uch a powerful form of
mental black magic.

Once you invite depression through an ‘‘automatic’’ se-
ries of cognitive distortions, your feelings and actions will
reinforce each oth

ctions will
reinforce each other in a self-perpetuating vicious cycle.
Because you believe whatever your depressed brain tells
you, you find yourself feeling negative about almost

feeling negative about almost every-
thing. This reaction occurs in milliseconds, too quickly for
you even to be aware of it. The negative emotion feels
realistic and in turn lends

ls
realistic and in turn lends an aura of credibility to the dis-
torted thought which created it. The cycle goes on and on,
and you are eventually trapped. The mental prison is an

apped. The mental prison is an
illusion, a hoax you have inadvertently created, but it seems
real because it feels real.

What is the key to releasing yourself from your emotional

yourself from your emotional
prison? Simply this: Your thoughts create your emotions;
therefore, your emotions cannot prove that your thoughts
are accurate. Unpleasant feelings me

curate. Unpleasant feelings merely indicate that you
are thinking something negative and believing it. Your emo-
tions follow your thoughts just as surely as baby ducks
follow thei

rely as baby ducks
follow their mother. But the fact that the baby ducks follow
faithfully along doesn’t prove that the mother knows where
she is going!

Let’s examine your equatio

g!

Let’s examine your equation, ‘‘I feel, therefore I am.’’
This attitude that emotions reflect a kind of self-evident,
ultimate truth is not unique to depressed people. Most psy-

to depressed people. Most psy-

represent emotio

m more openly
represent emotional maturity. The implication is that your

My position is quite d

stion.

My position is quite different. Your feelings, per se, are
not necessarily special at all. In fact, to the extent that your
negative emotions are based on mental distortion

sirable. I believe that once you have
learned how to perceive life more realistically you will ex-
perience an enhanced emotional life with a greater appre-
ciation for genuine sad

appre-
ciation for genuine sadness—which lacks distortion—as
well as joy.

he real question here is how to draw the

What is the difference between ‘‘healthy sadness’’ and

The distincti

and
depression?

The distinction is simple. Sadness is a normal emotion
created by realistic perceptions that describe a negative event
involving loss or disappointment in an undis

or disappointment in an undistorted way.

231

232 David D. Burns, M.D.

Depression 1s an illness that always results from thoughts
that are distorted in some way. For example, wh

d in some way. For example, when a loved
one dies, you validly think, ‘‘I lost him (or her), and I will
miss the companionship and love we shared.’’ The feelings
such a thought cre

he feelings
such a thought creates are tender, realistic, and desirable.
Your emotions will enhance your humanity and add depth
to the meaning of life. In this way you gain from yo

. In this way you gain from your loss.

In contrast, you might tell yourself, ‘‘I’ll never again be
happy because he (or she) died. It’s unfair!’’ These thoughts
will trigger in yo

se thoughts
will trigger in you feelings of self-pity and hopelessness.
Because these emotions are based entirely on distortion,
they will defeat you.

Either depression or sadness

Either depression or sadness can develop after a loss or
a failure in your efforts to reach a goal of great personal
importance. Sadness comes, however, without distortion.
It in

ver, without distortion.
It involves a flow of feeling and therefore has a time limit.
It never involves a lessening of your self-esteem. Depression
is frozen—it tends to persist o

s frozen—it tends to persist or recur indefinitely, and always
involves loss of self-esteem.

When a depression clearly appears after an obvious stress,
such as ill health, the dea

s,
such as ill health, the death of a loved one, or a business
reversal, it is sometimes called a “‘reactive depression.’’ At
times it can be more difficult to identify the stressf

ficult to identify the stressful event

‘‘endogenous’’ because the symptoms seem to be generated
entirely out of thin

enerated
entirely out of thin air. In both cases, however, the cause
of the depression is identical—your distorted, negative
thoughts. It has no adaptive or positive function whats

ive or positive function whatsoever,
and represents one of the worst forms of suffering. Its only
redeeming value is the growth you experience when you
recover from it.

My point i

u
recover from it.

My point is this: When a genuinely negative event occurs,

and perceptions. Your feelings will result

ns. Your feelings will result from the meaning

suffering will be due to the distortions in your thoughts.
When you elimina

our thoughts.
When you eliminate these distortions, you will find that
coping with the ‘“‘real problem’’ will become less painful.

selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors called paroxetine
(trade name Paxil).

aroxetine

abolize the new drug (desipramine) very well.

could develop a

Is this serious? Well, there are three potential problems.
First, desipramine is not effective at excessively high blood

ive at excessively high blood
levels. Second, there will be many more side effects at high
levels. And third, in rare instances, excessive blood levels
of desipramine can trigger a

of desipramine can trigger abnormal heart rhythms and oc-

Is this type of drug interaction rare? No. The levels of
antidepressants can sometimes inc

over-the-counter drugs you might take without thinking

hout thinking
twice. The tables in Chapter 20 will delineate the interac-

Finally, some toxic and dangerous drug in

me toxic and dangerous drug interactions do
not necessarily depend on doses or blood levels. For ex-
ample, many of the newer antidepressants such as Prozac
have powerful effects o

Prozac
have powerful effects on the serotonin systems in the brain.
The monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) also affect the
serotonin systems in the brain, but through a different

brain, but through a different
mechanism. The antidepressant tranylcypromine (trade
name Parnate) is an example of one of these MAOI drugs.
If you take Prozac and Parnate at the sa

bination could trigger an extremely dangerous reaction
known as the “‘serotonin syndrome.’’ The symptoms can

include fever, muscle rigidity, and rapid changes in blood
pressure, along with agitation, delirium, seizures, coma, and
death. Obviously, this combinatio

given!

You will see in Chapter 20 that many medications can
be dangerous if you are taking an MAOI. The list of for-
bidden

MAOI. The list of for-

gestants (especially if they contain dextromethorphan, a

uding

Some of these drugs will cause the serotonin syndrome de-

em will cause another dan-

extreme cases, the symptoms of a hypertensive crisis in-
clude brain hemorrhage, paralysis, coma,

list if you are taking one of the MAOIs, because they can

cause they can

(trade name Hismanal and used for

Costs of Antidepressant Medications

We often think that more expensive means better, but thi

xpensive means better, but this
is not always the case with antidepressants. As it turns out,
there are some very dramatic differences in the costs of the
different antidepressants

the
different antidepressants that do not reflect differences in ef-
fectiveness. In other words, sometimes a drug that is much
cheaper will be just as effective, or even more eff

as effective, or even more effective, than
another drug that costs more than forty times more.
Therefore, if the cost of the medication is a concern for you,
then a little educatio

or you,
then a little education may save you a great deal of money.

The costs and doses of the most commonly prescribed
antidepressants and mood stabilizing agents are listed in

discussions of drug costs.

Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)

Currently, the most popular antidepressant drugs are the
selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, or SSRIs. At this
time, f

ors, or SSRIs. At this
time, five SSRIs are prescribed in the United States. These
include citalopram (Celexa), the newest SSRI which was
released in the U.S. in 1998, fluoxetine (

the U.S. in 1998, fluoxetine (Prozac), the first
SSRI which was released in 1988, and fluvoxamine (Lu-
vox), paroxetine (Paxil), and sertraline (Zoloft). The effects
of these SSRIs

t). The effects
of these SSRIs on the brain are much more specific and
selective than the older tricyclic and tetracyclic drugs dis-
cussed above. Instead of interacting with many

tead of interacting with many different
systems in the brain, these drugs have selective effects on
nerves that use serotonin as a transmitter substance.

When it first appeared on

ce.

When it first appeared on the market, there was a great
deal of excitement about Prozac because it was chemically

548 David D. Burns, M.D.

quite different from the older ant

e different from the older antidepressants. Unlike the
tricyclic and tetracyclic drugs, it has specific effects on the
serotonergic nerves in the brain. Since a serotonin defi-
cie

n. Since a serotonin defi-
ciency was hypothesized to be the cause of depression, it
was hoped that Prozac would be dramatically more effec-
tive than the tricyclic and tetracyclic

the tricyclic and tetracyclic drugs which seemed
to affect so many different systems in the brain in a less
specific manner. It was also expected that Prozac (and the
other SSRIs)

t Prozac (and the
other SSRIs) would have fewer side effects than the tri-
cyclic and tetracyclic drugs. This is because Prozac does
not have such strong effects on the histaminic,

ong effects on the histaminic, alpha-
adrenergic, and muscarinic receptors.

Only one of these two hopes was fulfilled. Prozac and
the other four SSRIs do cause significantly fewer

s do cause significantly fewer side ef-
fects than the tricyclic and tetracyclic antidepressants and
are more pleasant to take. For example, they are less likely
to cause sleepines

less likely
to cause sleepiness, weight gain, dry mouth, dizziness, and
so on. They are also much safer since they are less likely
to have adverse effects on the heart, and they ar

ects on the heart, and they are much less
likely to result in death if a patient intentionally or unin-
tentionally takes an overdose. The biochemists who created
these new drugs d

who created
these new drugs deserve credit in this regard.

Unfortunately, the SSRIs are not more effective than the
older drugs. As many as 60 percent to 70 percent of de-
presse

nt to 70 percent of de-
pressed patients will improve when treated with SSRIs, and
these percentages are no better than the older drugs. Among
chronically depressed patients, the p

ally depressed patients, the probability of respond-
ing appears to be lower. The SSRIs also appear to be
slightly less effective than the older tricyclic antidepressants
for more

clic antidepressants
for more severely depressed patients. In addition, the
amount of improvement is often only partial—the patient
may become less depressed, but may not return to

pressed, but may not return to full self-
esteem and joyous daily living. This is a problem for all
the antidepressants, and not just the SSRIs. Although they
are no more effective

ugh they
are no more effective, the SSRIs are dramatically more ex-
pensive than the older drugs. In addition, the SSRIs have

The mechanism of action of the MAOIs is different from
that of the other antidepressant drugs. You learned in Chap-
ter

rugs. You learned in Chap-

epinephrine, or dopamine build up in the synaptic regions.
In contrast, the MAOIs seem to work by preventing the
breakdown

a result, levels of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine
build up inside the nerve terminals and these

are released into the synapses in much higher concentra-
tions when the nerves fire. This results in a greater stimu-
lation of the nerves

er stimu-
lation of the nerves at the other side of the synaptic
junctions.

Valproic acid is usually

Acid

Valproic acid is usually used in the treatment of epilepsy
but was recently granted FDA approval for the treatment
of bipolar disorder, especially acute mania. You can see in

ly acute mania. You can see in

expensive divalproex sodium fo

expensive divalproex sodium form (Depakote). The two
forms are equally effective. Studies comparing valproic
acid with lithium indicate that the two drugs are compara-
bly effectiv

rugs are compara-
bly effective and both appear to be twice as effective as a
placebo. Valproic acid, like lithium, also appears to be ef-
fective in preventing or reducing future

preventing or reducing future manic episodes.
The drug may be especially effective in the treatment of
the rapid-cycling form of bipolar disorder. It can help pa-
tients who experi

can help pa-
tients who experience mania and depression at the same
time (so-called ‘‘mixed states’’), as well as patients who
experience the more common forms of bipolar disorder.

mon forms of bipolar disorder. It
is probably less effective in the prevention and treatment
of depression than in the prevention and treatment of mania.

50 mg administered with a meal.

on 500 mg twic

on 500 mg twice a day.

inue with this approach. However, if a

If you have received an adequate dose of an antidepres-
sant for

pletely different class to maximize the chance of a positive
response. This idea makes good sense, since the different
antidepressants h

he different
antidepressants have slightly different effects on the brain.
If you have failed to respond to an SSRI such as fluoxetine
(Prozac), your doctor may want to try a tricy

doctor may want to try a tricyclic such as

mine has effects o

eas imipra-
mine has effects on many different systems.

If you switch to another drug, you will usually need to
taper off your current drug slowly so as to prevent any
withdrawal

as to prevent any
withdrawal effects. Antidepressants are not addictive and
they do not cause craving when you stop taking them.
However, they need to be discontinued slowly to pr

o be discontinued slowly to prevent
uncomfortable withdrawal reactions. For example, the tri-
cyclics can cause insomnia and upset stomach if you go off
them abruptly, as noted pre

ff
them abruptly, as noted previously.

Further, as noted above, there may be a mandatory wait-
ing period when you are switching from one drug to an-
other. This is because the tw

other. This is because the two drugs might be dangerous if
mixed together, and the effects of the first drug may persist
for a while after you have stopped taking it. The classic

topped taking it. The classic
example would be switching from an SSRI, such as fluox-
etine (Prozac), to an MAOIT, such as tranylcypromine (Par-
nate). The combination of these two

. The combination of these two drugs can cause the

previously described serotonin syndrome, which is occa-
sionally fatal. In addition, both types of drugs clear

body slowly, and so a drug-free period is necessary before

zac, an SSRI, to Parn

rom Pro-

Suppose that all these strategies fa

e that all these strategies fail to bring about an
optimal antidepressant response. What then? In my expe-
rience this is not unusual. I have seen lots of patients who
were treated

s of patients who
were treated for years with all kinds of medications and yet
they were still severely depressed. Early in my career, I
realized that drugs did not provide the ans

drugs did not provide the answer for many
people. That is why I devoted so much of my career to the
development of new psychotherapeutic techniques, such as
those described in thi

such as
those described in this book. I wanted to have more tools
available than just drugs.

In my experience, the idea that a pill alone will solve your
problems and bring you jo

your
problems and bring you joy is not productive. In contrast, the
willingness to use these cognitive therapy tools, often in
combination with a compassionate, persistent, and cre

assionate, persistent, and creative
therapist will often lead to substantial improvement.

Other Drugs Your Doctor May Prescribe

The various types of antidepressants I have descri

antidepressants I have described are
the ones that in my opinion have a clear-cut indication in
the treatment of depression. I will describe several types of
drugs that you might

types of
drugs that you might want to avoid, although there are ex-
ceptions to this rule.

Minor Tranquilizers [Benzodiazepines]. Some doctors
use minor tranquilizers (called benz

nor tranquilizers (called benzodiazepines) or seda-
tives to treat nervousness and anxiety. The benzodiazepines
include many familiar drugs such as alprazolam (Xanax),
chlordiazepo

prazolam (Xanax),
chlordiazepoxide (Librium), clonazepam (Klonopin), clor-

azepate (Tranxene), diazepam (Valium), lorazepam (Ati-
van), oxazepam (Serax)

(Ati-
van), oxazepam (Serax) and prazepam (Centrax). Minor
tranquilizers may be added to the mix of drugs your doctor
prescribes if you are depressed. Because most depressed
patie

. Because most depressed
patients also experience anxiety, this practice is unfortu-
nately quite common.

I usually do not recommend minor tranquilizers because

ers because

make your depression worse. In my experience, anxiety can

ted successfully without using these

ences Center, and Dr. Sherry H.

University, recently reviewed the world literature on the

nitive behavioral
therapy versus medications. Based on their careful review
of many clinical outcome studies, the authors recommended
treatment of anxiety disorders with cognitive

iety disorders with cognitive behavior ther-
apy instead of medications.’ The authors concluded that
cognitive behavioral therapy without drugs is a highly ef-
fective and long las

than usual to treat. However, you may respond nicely
to another ty

y respond nicely
to another type of treatment. I have treated large num-
bers of patients who had years and years of unsuc-
cessful treatment with a wide variety of drugs prior to

ide variety of drugs prior to
seeing me. Many of these ‘‘difficult’’ patients recov-
ered when I used cognitive therapy techniques like
those described in this book.

No single tre

d in this book.

No single treatment is a panacea for everyone. That’s
why it is important to have lots of approaches avail-
able, including many different kinds of medicines and

erent kinds of medicines and
many different kinds of psychotherapeutic methods as
well. The term, ‘‘different strokes for different folks’’
is right on target in the context of dep
